title: Lemon and poppy seed loaf
subtitle: A zesty, tender butter cake with poppy seeds and lemon drizzle
excerpt: A simple, reliable butter cake made with lemon zest and poppy seeds throughout, finished with a sharp lemon drizzle that soaks into the warm loaf and sets to a thin crust.
sourceNotes: Original recipe. The lemon drizzle loaf is a British baking staple without a single canonical source; the drizzle technique of soaking a still-hot cake with sugar syrup or lemon sugar appears in British baking literature from the mid-twentieth century. The poppy seed combination follows the Central European tradition of lemon-poppy seed baking.

content.text: About lemon and poppy seed loaf

This is one of the most reliable things to bake. It is a simple butter cake with no tricky steps. It turns out well from the first attempt. The poppy seeds add texture through the crumb. Lemon zest goes into the batter; a sharp lemon-sugar
poured over the warm loaf gives it a clear, bright acidity.

The drizzle step is important: it must be poured over the hot loaf while still in the tin. The syrup sinks into the crumb and the sugar crystals at the surface set as the loaf cools, forming the characteristic faintly gritty top crust. Pouring it over a cold loaf leaves the glaze sitting on top rather than penetrating.

attrs.prepNote: finely grated zest of both, juice of both for the drizzle

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C fan (180°C conventional). Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about four minutes. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time. If the mixture curdles, add a spoonful of flour. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}}, the lemon zest from {{lemon}}, and {{poppy-seeds}} until just combined. Spoon into the prepared tin and level the surface.

Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the surface is a uniform golden-brown with a crack down the centre. An oven thermometer helps; an oven that runs hot will dome and crack the top before the centre is set.

As soon as the loaf comes out of the oven, prick the surface all over with a skewer. Mix the juice from both {{lemon}} with {{granulated-sugar}} and stir briefly. Pour the mixture slowly over the hot loaf still in its tin. Leave to cool completely in the tin before turning out; this lets the syrup distribute evenly and the top crust set.
Granulated sugar in the drizzle (not caster) gives a slightly coarser texture on the top crust. Both work; granulated is more pronounced.
Adding 2 tablespoons of natural yoghurt to the batter gives a slightly more tender, moist crumb.
The loaf improves on the second day after baking; the drizzle distributes more evenly through the crumb.
